explanation_md: `r` is an alias for `x`. Assigning `20` through `r` changes the single underlying object. Both `x` and `r` now refer to the same location holding `20`, so the output is `20 20`.

options.a: `a` is 1 — the assignment `r = b` rebinds `r` to `b`, leaving `a` unchanged.
options.b: `a` is 9 — `r = b` copies the value of `b` into the object that `r` aliases (which is `a`).
options.c: `a` is undefined — assigning through a reference after initialization is illegal.
options.d: `a` is 9 only if `b` was declared before `a`.

explanation_md: References cannot be reseated. `r = b` assigns the *value* of `b` (which is `9`) to the object `r` aliases, which is `a`. After this line `a == 9` and `r` still aliases `a`. References can never be redirected to a different object.

options.a: `100` is an rvalue (a literal); a non-const lvalue reference cannot bind to an rvalue.
options.b: The reference `r` must be declared with `auto&` to bind to an integer literal.
options.c: The program compiles but prints nothing because `r` has no value.
options.d: `int& r` should be `int r` — references cannot be initialized with brace syntax.

explanation_md: A non-const lvalue reference (`int&`) requires its initializer to be an lvalue — an object with a stable address. `100` is an integer literal, which is an rvalue with no persistent address. The fix is either to use a named variable (`int x { 100 }; int& r { x };`) or to make the reference `const int&`.

`addTen` takes its parameter as a non-const lvalue reference, so `n` is an alias for `val`. The line `n = n + 10` adds 10 to `val` directly. After `addTen` returns, `val` is `17`, which is what gets printed.

prompt_md: Which of these statements about lvalue references is TRUE?

options.a: A reference can be left uninitialized and bound to an object later.
options.b: A reference can be rebound to a different object after initialization by assigning through it.
options.c: A reference must be initialized at the point of declaration and always aliases the same object for its lifetime.
options.d: A reference stores a copy of the value it was initialized with.

explanation_md: References must be initialized when declared and cannot be reseated. They are permanent aliases: the reference will refer to the same object for its entire lifetime. Assigning through a reference changes the *value of the aliased object*, not which object the reference aliases.
